era_context: The Late Bronze Age collapse is in its final stages or recently concluded (c. 1200–1150 BCE). The Iron Age is emerging across the Mediterranean and Near East. Egypt's New Kingdom is in its final phase under Ramesses III or his immediate successors. The Zhou Dynasty in China is either in its final stages of conquest or recently established. The Steppe, Europe, and most of the world outside the Mediterranean and East Asia have no written historical record for this period.

events.title: Late Bronze Age collapse aftermath; Iron Age consolidation in the Mediterranean
events.description: The catastrophic disruptions of the Late Bronze Age collapse (c. 1200–1150 BCE) have largely concluded. Populations are resettling, new political structures are forming, and Iron Age technologies are spreading. The palatial systems of the Bronze Age have collapsed; smaller, more decentralised polities are emerging. Trade networks are being re-established on new foundations. This is a period of significant cultural and technological transition, with considerable regional variation in recovery and adaptation.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from multiple Mediterranean sites showing settlement discontinuity and Iron Age transitions

events.certainty_note: The Late Bronze Age collapse is a process spanning roughly 1200–1150 BCE, not a single-year event. The year 1002 BCE falls within the aftermath period, but no specific events are documented for this exact year.

events.title: Egyptian Third Intermediate Period; political fragmentation
events.description: Egypt enters the Third Intermediate Period (c. 1070–664 BCE), characterised by the fragmentation of central authority. The priesthood of Amun at Thebes becomes increasingly autonomous and wealthy, effectively ruling Upper Egypt as a theocratic state. Lower Egypt remains under nominal pharaonic control from the Delta. This period sees the rise of Libyan mercenaries and settlers, who gradually integrate into Egyptian society and eventually establish their own dynasties. The unified New Kingdom state has effectively ceased to exist.
key_figures: Ramesses XI (last New Kingdom pharaoh, r. c. 1099–1069 BCE)

sources.name: Papyrus records from the Third Intermediate Period

events.certainty_note: The transition to the Third Intermediate Period is dated to c. 1070 BCE, placing it approximately 68 years after -1001. The exact year of transition is debated; some scholars place it as late as 1050 BCE.

disconfirming_evidence: No specific events are documented for the year 1002 BCE. The events listed represent broader processes occurring across this century. The dating of the Late Bronze Age collapse and the Egyptian transition to the Third Intermediate Period are both subject to scholarly debate, with variations of ±20–50 years depending on the chronological system used. The 'low chronology' for Egypt would shift these dates significantly later.
historiographic_note: The year 1002 BCE falls in a period of significant transition but almost no year-specific documentation. Written records are fragmentary and heavily concentrated in Egypt and China. The historical record is dominated by elite perspectives (pharaohs, priests, kings); the experiences of ordinary populations are largely invisible. Archaeological evidence provides material culture and settlement patterns but cannot be dated to individual years with precision. Most of the world has no written historical record for this period.
